seo.title: GDPR Compliance Checklist for SaaS Apps
seo.description: A practical GDPR compliance checklist for SaaS developers. Cover data collection, consent, retention, and breach notification requirements.

content.intro: GDPR compliance is not a one-time task — it's an ongoing operational requirement. This checklist covers the key areas SaaS developers must address when processing EU personal data. Work through each section systematically; mark critical items before launch and revisit recommended items in your first sprint post-launch.

items.title: Identify the legal basis for every data processing activity
items.description: Document whether each processing activity relies on consent, legitimate interest, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, or public task.

items.title: Implement granular consent for non-essential cookies and tracking
items.description: Consent banners must offer genuine choice — pre-checked boxes or bundled consent are non-compliant. Use a CMP like Cookiebot or Termly.

items.title: Store consent records with timestamps and version of the privacy policy shown
items.description: You must be able to prove when a user consented and what they agreed to. Log consent events in your database.

items.title: Provide a simple, accessible mechanism to withdraw consent
items.description: Withdrawing consent must be as easy as giving it. A visible link in every email footer and in account settings is the minimum.

items.title: Review legitimate interest claims with a balancing test
items.description: Legitimate interest requires a three-part test: purpose, necessity, and balancing. Document this for each LI claim.

items.title: Write a privacy policy in plain language — avoid legal boilerplate
items.description: Explain clearly: what data you collect, why, how long you keep it, who you share it with, and how users can exercise their rights.

items.title: Include a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with every third-party processor
items.description: Any vendor that processes EU personal data on your behalf requires a DPA. Common examples: analytics tools, email providers, hosting.

items.title: Add a cookie policy listing every cookie and its purpose
items.description: Categorize cookies as necessary, functional, analytics, or marketing. List third-party cookies from embedded scripts.

items.title: Display a privacy notice at every data collection point
items.description: Forms, sign-up flows, and survey links should have a brief notice explaining what data is collected and the legal basis.

items.title: Date your privacy policy and notify users when it changes materially
items.description: Keep a changelog or version history. Email notice for material changes to terms is best practice (and sometimes required).

items.title: Implement a self-serve data export feature (right to portability)
items.description: Users can request a machine-readable export of their data. Build an export endpoint that outputs JSON or CSV — don't rely on manual processes.

items.title: Build an account deletion flow that purges all personal data (right to erasure)
items.description: Deletion must propagate to all data stores: primary DB, caches, backups (on schedule), analytics, and third-party processors.

items.title: Create an internal process for handling data subject requests within 30 days
items.description: GDPR mandates response within one month. Document who handles requests, how they verify identity, and how they fulfill each type.

items.title: Provide a contact method for data-related requests in your privacy policy
items.description: An email address like privacy@yourdomain.com is sufficient. A web form with an SLA is better.

items.title: Test your deletion flow to confirm data is actually removed
items.description: Run test user creation and deletion. Check your database, analytics, and email provider to verify data is gone.

items.title: Encrypt personal data at rest and in transit
items.description: TLS for all connections. Encrypted database fields for sensitive data (PII, payment info). Document your encryption approach.

items.title: Define and enforce a data retention policy
items.description: Data should not be kept longer than necessary. Set automatic deletion schedules for inactive accounts, logs, and analytics data.

items.title: Implement access controls and audit logs for who accesses personal data
items.description: Not every employee needs access to production user data. Role-based access controls and access logs demonstrate compliance.

items.title: Have a documented data breach notification procedure
items.description: GDPR requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering a breach. Know your local DPA and have a template ready.

items.title: Review and minimize the personal data you collect
items.description: Data minimization is a GDPR principle. Audit your sign-up forms and analytics — every unnecessary field is a liability.

items.title: Audit all third-party services for GDPR compliance and DPA availability
items.description: Create a data processing register listing every vendor, the data shared, the legal basis, and where data is processed.

items.title: For US-based vendors, verify they rely on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or adequacy decisions
items.description: The EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) provides an adequacy decision. Verify vendor certification at dataprivacyframework.gov.

items.title: Replace non-compliant analytics with a privacy-first alternative
items.description: Google Analytics requires consent banners for most EU use cases. Plausible, Fathom, or Umami can work without consent banners in many cases.

items.title: Review your email provider's data processing location and DPA
items.description: If your email provider processes data in the US, ensure SCCs are in place. Consider EU-based alternatives like Brevo or Mailcoach.

items.title: Document your data flows in a Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)
items.description: A RoPA is required for organizations of 250+ employees, but recommended for all. It's your audit evidence if the DPA investigates.
